title: Watercolour journal backgrounds
subtitle: Painting flat, gradient, and wet-in-wet washes as journal page backgrounds
excerpt: A watercolour background turns a plain journal page into a toned field that unifies everything written or drawn on top. This tutorial covers three background types: the flat wash, the graded wash, and the wet-in-wet colour drop. Each takes under five minutes per spread and dries to a stable surface that accepts pen, pencil, and ink.
sourceNotes: Standard watercolour technique, synthesised from Jean Haines, Watercolour for the Absolute Beginner (2011, Search Press), wash chapter. Applied to journalling contexts following widely-documented bullet journal and art journal practice.

content.text: Plain cartridge at 220 gsm handles light watercolour washes well, the key is using enough water so the colour flows, but not so much that the paper buckles badly before drying. Hot-press watercolour paper is a better surface but not necessary for journal backgrounds. If the journal pages cockle badly after washing, press the open journal face-down under a heavy book for 20 minutes after the wash dries.

Pre-mix enough colour to cover the full area: load a generous amount of one pan colour with water into a small mixing cup. Tilt the journal slightly so the wash will pool at the bottom as you work. Apply the first stroke across the top of the page with a wide, loaded brush. Keep the brush full: each stroke should pick up the bead of colour pooling at the bottom of the previous stroke. Work from top to bottom without lifting the brush mid-stroke. A
must be completed before any area begins to dry, or tide marks will appear.

Start the same as a flat wash, but after each stroke add a little more water to the brush and a little less colour from the pan. Each successive stroke is therefore slightly more diluted. End the last stroke with near-clean water. A
works well as a horizon background for text: the deepest colour at the top grades to almost white at the bottom, leaving the lower half of the page clean for writing.

Pre-wet the entire page with clear water on a wide brush. While the surface is still shiny, drop concentrated colour from a loaded brush tip at intervals across the wet surface. The colour spreads and blooms into the water in soft irregular shapes. Add a second colour drop near the first, where the two colours meet, they blend.
backgrounds are unpredictable; that is the point. Let them dry fully (20-30 minutes) before writing on top.
